Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". - simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). 44				return err
45			}
46			return nil
47		default:

49		}
50	}

3:The goal of this file is to have a place to easily commit answers to questions
33:The [Machine API Operator](https://github.com/openshift/machine-api-operator) manages "machine" objects which represent underlying IaaS virtual ([or physical](https://github.com/openshift-metal3)) machines.
45:If for example you write a daemonset that writes a custom systemd unit into e.g. `/etc/systemd/system`, or do so manually via `ssh`/`oc debug node` - OS upgrades will preserve that change (via libostree), and the MCO will not revert it.  The MCO/MCD only changes files included in `MachineConfigs`, there is no code to look for "unknown" files.
49:Stated more generally, on an OSTree managed system, all content in `/etc` and `/var` is [preserved by default across upgrades](https://ostreedev.github.io/ostree/adapting-existing/).
53:If a file that *is* managed by MachineConfig is changed, the MCD will detect this and go degraded.  We go degraded rather than overwrite in order to avoid [reboot loops](https://github.com/openshift/machine-config-operator/pull/245).
55:In the future, we would like to harden things more so that these things are more controlled, and ideally avoid having any persistent "unmanaged" state.  But it will take significant work to get there; and the status quo means that we can support other operators such as SDN (and e.g. [nmstate](https://github.com/nmstate/kubernetes-nmstate)) that may control parts of the host without the MCO's awareness.
59:In clusters that are managed by the [Machine API](https://github.com/openshift/machine-api-operator/), see
60:[this question](https://github.com/openshift/machine-api-operator/blob/master/FAQ.md#i-created-a-machine-but-it-never-joined-the-cluster)
66:[this issue](https://github.com/coreos/ignition/issues/585).
91: - https://github.com/openshift/machine-config-operator/issues/1619
92: - https://github.com/openshift/machine-config-operator/pull/2035
107:Or, adding the RHEL UBI repository or [full RHEL entitlements](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/4.13/support/remote_health_monitoring/insights-operator-simple-access.html#insights-operator-simple-access) may work.
112:to instead switch to an [image layering](https://docs.openshift.com/container-platform/4.13/post_installation_configuration/coreos-layering.html) model.
116:Yes, RHEL worker nodes will have a instance of the Machine Config Daemon running on them.  However, only a subset of MCO functionality is supported on RHEL worker nodes.  It is possible to create a Machine Config to write files and `systemd` units to RHEL worker nodes, but it is not possible to manage OS updates, kernel arguments, or extensions on RHEL worker nodes.

1	# MachineConfig
2	
3	## Goal
4	
5	1. MachineConfig objects should be complete source of machine configuration at install / first-boot for a machine.
6	
7	2. MachineConfig objects should be the source of machine configuration during upgrades.
8	
9	3. The MachineConfig served to the machines should be static and there should be no links to remote locations for dynamic configuration. This includes both remote sources for Ignition and remote files.
10	
11	4. Define a way to merge multiple MachineConfigs.
12	
13	## Non Goals
14	
15	1. Updates from the remote sources for Ignition config will not be actively applied.
16	
17	## Overview
18	
19	The machines using `RHCOS` will be configured using an Ignition config served to the machines at first boot. An in-cluster Ignition endpoint will serve these Ignition configs to new machines based on the MachineConfig object defined.
20	Also during upgrades the MachineConfigDaemon running on these machines will use the defined MachineConfig object to upgrade the machine's configurations.
21	
22	Users will not be allowed to change the MachineConfig object defined by openshift. Although, users will create new MachineConfig objects for their customization. Therefore the MachineConfig object used by the in-cluster Ignition server and daemon running on the machines has to be a merged version.
23	
24	The MachineConfig object used by in-cluster Ignition server and daemon running on the machine should be a static definition of all the resources. Users might create MachineConfig objects that have links to remote Ignition configs, but at merge time, a snapshot of the remote Ignition config should be used to create the merged MachineConfig. The same is valid for remote files, at merge time the files are fetched and the contents are verified and replaced in-place the final merged MachineConfig.
25	
26	## Detailed Design
27	
28	### Final Rendered MachineConfig object
29	
30	MachineConfig objects can be created by both the OpenShift platform and users to define machine configurations.  There is a final "rendered" MachineConfig object (prefixed with `rendered-`) that is the union of its inputs.
31	
32	1. The rendered MachineConfig object contains merged spec of all the different MachineConfig objects that are valid for the machine.
33	
34	2. To ensure the configuration does not change unexpectedly between usage, all remote content referenced in the ignition config is retrieved and embedded into the merged MachineConfig at the time of generation.
35	
36	### No remote sources in rendered MachineConfig
37	
38	To ensure all the machines see the same configurations, remote sources need to be resolved to a snapshot at generation time.
39	
40	### MachineConfig definition
41	
42	```go
43	type MachineConfig struct {
44	    metav1.TypeMeta `json:",inline"`
45	    metav1.ObjectMeta `json:"metadata,omitempty"`
46	
47	    Spec MachineConfigSpec `json:spec`
48	}
49	
50	type MachineConfigSpec struct {
51	    // Config is a Ignition Config object.
52	    Config ign.Config `json:"config"`
53	    KernelArguments []string `json:"kernelArguments"`
54	    Extensions      []string `json:"extensions"`
55	    Fips bool `json:"fips"`
56	    KernelType string `json:"kernelType"`
57	}
58	```
59	
60	The actual custom resource manifest then could look like this if you are applying it to cluster on version >= 4.7:
61	
62	```
63	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
64	kind: MachineConfig
65	metadata:
66	  name: worker-2864988432
67	spec:
68	  config:
69	    ignition:
70	      version: 3.2.0
71	    storage:
72	      files:
73	      - contents:
74	          source: data:,%20
75	        mode: 384
76	        path: /root/myfile
77	```
78	
79	and like this if you are applying it to a cluster on version < 4.6:
80	
81	```
82	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
83	kind: MachineConfig
84	metadata:
85	  name: worker-2864988432
86	spec:
87	  config:
88	    ignition:
89	      version: 2.2.0
90	    storage:
91	      files:
92	      - contents:
93	          source: data:,%20
94	        filesystem: root
95	        mode: 384
96	        path: /root/myfile
97	```
98	
99	Notice how it's the usual Ignition config object *inplace*, not as a JSON
100	string -- also note the casing follows the `json:` markers in the definition above, of course this follows for the
101	Ignition config keys as well.
102	
103	### How to create generated MachineConfig
104	
105	1. For each MachineConfig object,
106	
107	    a. Use the Ignition's `internal` package to render a valid Ignition config. Ignition render fetches remote sources and then appends/replaces inline config. [More Info](https://github.com/coreos/Ignition/blob/99b8d5052db6b30fe4812d2efbc8713a3abbef70/internal/exec/engine.go#L204-L252)
108	
109	    b. Ignition does not fetch remote sources for files in render. Run [fetcher](https://github.com/coreos/Ignition/blob/99b8d5052db6b30fe4812d2efbc8713a3abbef70/internal/exec/util/file.go#L183) separately to load the remote files.
110	
111	2. Use Ignition's [append](https://github.com/coreos/ignition/blob/99b8d5052db6b30fe4812d2efbc8713a3abbef70/config/v2_2/append.go#L23-L34) to merge all the Ignition configs generated above.
112	
113	    * Use the openshift defined Ignition config as base and append all the other Ignition configs in a pre-defined order.
114	
115	### KernelArguments
116	
117	This extends the host's kernel arguments.  Use this for e.g. [nosmt](https://access.redhat.com/solutions/rhel-smt).
118	As of OpenShift 4.3, you can set this field for either "day 1" (install time) or "day 2" configuration
119	once a cluster has been created. See [installer docs](https://github.com/openshift/installer/blob/master/docs/user/customization.md#nodes-with-custom-kernel-arguments) on how to set them as install-time MachineConfig objects. For "day 2" operation you can also add them as a similar machineconfig, incurring a reboot.
120	
121	Example MachineConfig to change kernel log level:
122	```
123	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
124	kind: MachineConfig
125	metadata:
126	  labels:
127	    machineconfiguration.openshift.io/role: "master"
128	  name: 99-master-kargs-loglevel
129	spec:
130	  kernelArguments:
131	    - 'loglevel=7'
132	```
133	
134	Note that for 4.2 clusters this is only supported as a "day 2" operation.
135	
136	#### Known Issue Affecting 4.2 Clusters
137	On a 4.2 based OCP cluster if we already have kernel arguments applied using MachineConfig and then we try to create a new node using openshift-machine-api, existing kargs won't get applied. This behaviour is because 4.2 doesn't know how to process kernel arguments during firstboot on a newly spun node. See [bug#1766346](https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=1766346) for more information.
138	
139	The best way to fix this issue is by getting the bootimage updated to 4.3 or later version which can be tracked at [enhancement proposal](https://github.com/openshift/enhancements/pull/201/) .
140	
141	Until we have the enhancement implemented it can be done manually by updating the bootimage in machineset.
142	
143	**Example:**
144	
145	Suppose we have a 4.2 cluster created on AWS and have applied karg `foo` to the cluster with the following MachineConfig:
146	```
147	$ cat worker-karg.yaml
148	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
149	kind: MachineConfig
150	metadata:
151	  labels:
152	    machineconfiguration.openshift.io/role: worker
153	  name: worker-kargs
154	spec:
155	  kernelArguments:
156	    - foo
157	```
158	The first step is to upgrade the cluster to OCP 4.3 or later version. Once the cluster has been successfully upgraded, list the available machinesets:
159	```
160	$ oc get machineset -n openshift-machine-api
161	NAME                                          DESIRED   CURRENT   READY   AVAILABLE   AGE
162	ci-ln-bx6fgqt-d5d6b-89fn7-worker-us-east-1b   2         2         2       2           79m
163	ci-ln-bx6fgqt-d5d6b-89fn7-worker-us-east-1c   1         1         1       1           79m
164	```
165	
166	In our example, let's edit the bootimage in machineset ci-ln-bx6fgqt-d5d6b-89fn7-worker-us-east-1c with corresponding 4.3 bootimage. For 4.4, a list of bootimages for different platforms and regions are available [here](https://github.com/openshift/installer/blob/release-4.4/data/data/rhcos.json), checkout the respective OCP release branch in the repo for the corresponding release. Since the cluster was created in AWS in the us-east-1 region, we will update the ami id to [ami-0543fbfb4749f3c3b](https://github.com/openshift/installer/blob/release-4.4/data/data/rhcos.json#L43) with the following command:
167	
168	```
169	$ oc edit machineset ci-ln-bx6fgqt-d5d6b-89fn7-worker-us-east-1c -n openshift-machine-api
170	 ```
171	Similarly, we can update the bootimage for the rest of the machinesets too.
172	Once the bootimage has been updated, create a node by scaling up machineset replicas.
173	```
174	$ oc scale --replicas=2 machineset ci-ln-9jk9j3b-d5d6b-kw7lr-worker-us-east-1c -n openshift-machine-api
175	```
176	
177	#### nosmt
178	When a machine boots with `nosmt` Kernel Argument, it disables multi-threading on that host and the system will only utilize physical CPU cores. While applying `nosmt` on any node in the cluster, ensure that enough CPU resources are available to schedule all pods, otherwise it can lead to a degraded cluster. For example: a basic 3 master and 3 worker node cluster having 2 physical CPU cores on each node should be fine.
179	
180	### KernelType
181	
182	This feature is available with OCP 4.4 and onward releases as both `day 1` and `day 2` operation. It allows to choose between traditional and Real Time (RT) kernel on an RHCOS node. Supported values are
183	`""` or `default` for traditional kernel, `realtime` for RT kernel and `64k-pages` for 64k memory pages on aarch64.
184	Note that `64k-pages` and `realtime` cannot be selected at the same time. Also, 64k pages support is limited to aarch64 architecture.
185	
186	To set kernelType field during cluster install, see the [installer guide](https://github.com/openshift/installer/blob/master/docs/user/customization.md#Switching-RHCOS-host-kernel-using-KernelType).
187	
188	For day 2, create a MachineConfig and apply to the cluster using `oc create -f`
189	
190	Example MachineConfig to switch to RT kernel on worker nodes:
191	```
192	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
193	kind: MachineConfig
194	metadata:
195	  labels:
196	    machineconfiguration.openshift.io/role: "worker"
197	  name: worker-kerneltype
198	spec:
199	  kernelType: realtime
200	```
201	
202	**Note:** The RT kernel lowers throughput (performance) in return for improved worst-case latency bounds. This feature is intended only for use cases that require consistent low latency. For more information, see the [Linux Foundation wiki](https://wiki.linuxfoundation.org/realtime/start) and the [RHEL RT portal](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux_for_real_time/8/).
203	
204	### RHCOS Extensions
205	RHCOS is a minimal OCP focused OS which provides capabilities common across all the platforms. With extensions support, OCP 4.6 and onward users can enable a limited set of additional functionality on the RHCOS nodes.
206	| OCP version   |     Supported extensions     |
207	| ------------- | ---------------------------- |
208	| 4.6           |  `usbguard`                  |
209	| 4.8           |  `usbguard`, `sandboxed-containers`    |
210	| 4.11          |  `usbguard`, `sandboxed-containers`, `kerberos`   |
211	| 4.14          |  `usbguard`, `sandboxed-containers`, `kerberos`, `ipsec`, `wasm`   |
212	| 4.18          |  `usbguard`, `sandboxed-containers`, `kerberos`, `ipsec`, `wasm` , `sysstat`   |
213	| 4.19          |  `usbguard`, `sandboxed-containers`, `kerberos`, `ipsec`, `wasm` , `sysstat` , `two-node-ha`   |
214	
215	Extensions can be installed by creating a MachineConfig object. Extensions can be enabled as both day1 and day2. Check [installer guide](https://github.com/openshift/installer/blob/master/docs/user/customization.md#Enabling-RHCOS-Extensions) to enable extensions during cluster install.
216	
217	Example MachineConfig to install usbguard on an existing cluster on worker nodes:
218	```
219	apiVersion: machineconfiguration.openshift.io/v1
220	kind: MachineConfig
221	metadata:
222	  labels:
223	    machineconfiguration.openshift.io/role: worker
224	  name: worker-extensions
225	spec:
226	  config:
227	    ignition:
228	      version: 3.2.0
229	  extensions:
230	    - usbguard
231	```
232	
233	### FIPS
234	
235	This allows to enable/disable [FIPS mode](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/7/html/security_guide/chap-federal_standards_and_regulations). If any of the configuration has FIPS enabled, it'll be set.  A similar restriction applies to this as for `KernelArguments` above.
236	
237	Enabling FIPS mode is a Day 1 operation, set at install time.  You cannot enable FIPS via a MachineConfig as a Day2 operation.
238	
239	### OSImageURL
240	
241	You should not attempt to set this field; it is controlled by the operator and injected directly into the final `rendered-` config.
242	For more information, see [OSUpgrades.md](OSUpgrades.md).
243

1	# once-from
2	
3	The promise of the next-generation OpenShift installer and Red Hat CoreOS
4	combined with the machine-config-operator is fully automated management
5	of the hosts in a highly integrated fashion.
6	
7	However, we also aim to support a "bring your own host" model. We want to avoid
8	duplication as much as possible between these two code paths. The idea of the
9	"once-from" mode for the machine-config-daemon is that one can pass it an
10	Ignition config (as generated by the installer for example) and execute it
11	on a host that was already created. (The feature also supports MachineConfig
12	manifests which are meant to be used for host updates rather than installation).
13	
14	This is mostly about laying down files and systemd units and the like; we
15	don't expect "once-from" to e.g. create users.
16	
17	# Testing once-from mode
18	
19	Once-from should generally be used on a machine not connected to a cluster.  You can use for example a traditional CentOS 7 machine;
20	this could be anything from a cloud instance to a Vagrant Box or even
21	a physical install.
22	
23	This guide was written using Vagrant to make a CentOS 7 box: https://app.vagrantup.com/centos/boxes/7
24	
25	An easy way to test this is to copy over the machine-config-daemon
26	binary from `_output/linux/amd64/machine-config-daemon` to the
27	target.  (With Vagrant for example, copy to the target dir then `vagrant rsync`, the output will be in `/vagrant`).
28	
29	To run:
30	
31	`./machine-config-daemon start --node-name $(hostname) --root-mount / --once-from $(pwd)/example.ign`
32	
33	Where `example.ign` here is the content from https://github.com/coreos/ignition/blob/master/doc/examples.md#start-services
34	
35	```json
36	{
37	  "ignition": { "version": "2.2.0" },
38	  "systemd": {
39	    "units": [{
40	      "name": "example.service",
41	      "enabled": true,
42	      "contents": "[Service]\nType=oneshot\nExecStart=/usr/bin/echo Hello World\n\n[Install]\nWantedBy=multi-user.target"
43	    }]
44	  }
45	}
46	```
47	
48	You can also try out the MachineConfig support of "once-from" mode by passing a MC manifest instead, see [HACKING.md](./HACKING.md) for a MachineConfig example.
49

/home/jsuri/random-testing/prashanth-help/machine-config-operator/docs/MachineConfig.md:9:3. The MachineConfig served to the machines should be static and there should be no links to remote locations for dynamic configuration. This includes both remote sources for Ignition and remote files.
/home/jsuri/random-testing/prashanth-help/machine-config-operator/docs/MachineConfig.md:15:1. Updates from the remote sources for Ignition config will not be actively applied.
/home/jsuri/random-testing/prashanth-help/machine-config-operator/docs/MachineConfig.md:24:The MachineConfig object used by in-cluster Ignition server and daemon running on the machine should be a static definition of all the resources. Users might create MachineConfig objects that have links to remote Ignition configs, but at merge time, a snapshot of the remote Ignition config should be used to create the merged MachineConfig. The same is valid for remote files, at merge time the files are fetched and the contents are verified and replaced in-place the final merged MachineConfig.
/home/jsuri/random-testing/prashanth-help/machine-config-operator/docs/MachineConfig.md:36:### No remote sources in rendered MachineConfig
/home/jsuri/random-testing/prashanth-help/machine-config-operator/docs/MachineConfig.md:38:To ensure all the machines see the same configurations, remote sources need to be resolved to a snapshot at generation time.
/home/jsuri/random-testing/prashanth-help/machine-config-operator/docs/MachineConfig.md:107:    a. Use the Ignition's `internal` package to render a valid Ignition config. Ignition render fetches remote sources and then appends/replaces inline config. [More Info](https://github.com/coreos/Ignition/blob/99b8d5052db6b30fe4812d2efbc8713a3abbef70/internal/exec/engine.go#L204-L252)
/home/jsuri/random-testing/prashanth-help/machine-config-operator/docs/MachineConfig.md:109:    b. Ignition does not fetch remote sources for files in render.
